questions.specification:
Ecologists monitored the development of an ecosystem on a newly formed volcanic island over a period of 150 years. The study tracked changes in biomass and energy flow as the community underwent primary succession.

| Stage | Years since formation | Total Biomass ($kg\ m^{-2}$) | Gross Primary Productivity ($g\ m^{-2}\ yr^{-1}$) | Respiration ($g\ m^{-2}\ yr^{-1}$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pioneer | 10 | 0.2 | 150 | 90 |
| Intermediate | 50 | 4.5 | 800 | 450 |
| Climax | 150 | 18.2 | 1200 | 1150 |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D4.2 Sustainability and change > D4.2.13 Changes occurring during primary succession (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D4.2.13 Changes occurring during primary succession (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question is explicitly about primary succession on a newly formed volcanic island, tracking biomass changes across pioneer/intermediate/climax stages and asking about how conditions change during succession.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C - Interaction and Interdependence > C4.2 Transfer of energy and matter > C4.2.15 Primary production as accumulation of carbon compounds in biomass by autotrophs
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C4.2.15 Primary production as accumulation of carbon compounds in biomass by autotrophs
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Calculating NPP from GPP and respiration and discussing changing energy storage as climax is approached directly tests primary production/biomass accumulation concepts.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D4.2 Sustainability and change > D4.2.15 Climax communities and arrested succession (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D4.2.15 Climax communities and arrested succession (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: References to the ecosystem approaching a climax community and comparison of energy flow/respiration in the climax stage align with climax community characteristics.

parts.content: State the relationship between time and total biomass during this process of primary succession.
parts.markscheme:
- Total biomass increases with time during succession (positive correlation). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Biomass is lowest in the pioneer stage and highest in the climax stage (increasing trend across stages). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the net primary productivity (NPP) for the intermediate stage. Then discuss how energy storage (biomass accumulation/NPP) changes from the intermediate stage to the climax community.
parts.markscheme:
- NPP calculation: $\text{NPP} = \text{GPP} - R = 800 - 450 = 350\ g\ m^{-2}\ yr^{-1}$. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Correct units stated for NPP: $g\ m^{-2}\ yr^{-1}$. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- From intermediate to climax, respiration increases substantially (higher community maintenance costs). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In the climax stage, $\text{GPP} \approx R$ (e.g. $1200$ vs $1150$), so NPP is low and little additional energy is stored as new biomass. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Biomass is still high in the climax community because it has accumulated over time, even though net biomass gain per year is small. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how pioneer species, such as lichens and mosses, alter the abiotic environment to allow for the establishment of later successional stages.
parts.markscheme:
- Physical and/or chemical weathering: pioneer organisms break down bare rock to produce smaller particles. <marks>1 mark</marks>
_**OR**_
- Organic matter/humus addition: death and decomposition of pioneers adds organic material, improving water retention and soil structure. <marks>1 mark</marks>
_**OR**_
- Nutrient enrichment: some pioneers (e.g. cyanobacteria in lichens) fix nitrogen, increasing soil fertility. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Award any two of the above points. <marks>2 marks</marks>

parts.content: Outline the adaptations of leaves that assist in water conservation in xerophytes.
parts.markscheme:
- Thick waxy cuticle reduces water loss from the leaf surface <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduced leaf surface area or leaves modified into spines minimize transpiration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sunken stomata trap moist air and reduce the water vapor potential gradient <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Stomata located on the leaf undersides are protected from direct sun and heat <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Succulent leaves are modified to store water <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Leaf hairs (trichomes) trap a layer of humid air and/or reduce air movement at the leaf surface, lowering transpiration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Rolled leaves reduce the exposed stomatal area and trap humid air <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award marks for any valid adaptations related to water conservation in leaves.</note>

parts.content: Describe how the root systems of xerophytes are adapted for water acquisition and conservation.
parts.markscheme:
- Deep roots reach water tables deep underground <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Extensive shallow roots allow for rapid absorption of surface water after rainfall <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Roots with specialized tissues allow for water storage <marks>1 mark</marks>
- High density of root hairs increases the surface area for osmosis <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Maintenance of high solute concentration in roots creates osmotic pressure for water uptake <marks>1 mark</marks>
- High root:shoot ratio (proportionally more biomass in roots) increases capacity to explore soil for water <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Mycorrhizal associations increase effective surface area for water (and mineral ion) uptake <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award marks for any valid points related to how roots aid in water conservation.</note>

parts.content: Explain how structural modifications of stems help xerophytes conserve water.
parts.markscheme:
- Thick and fleshy (succulent) stems store water during periods of drought <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Low surface area to volume ratio of stems reduces water loss <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Thick waxy cuticle on stems reduces evaporation from the stem surface <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ribbed or grooved surfaces allow for stem expansion when water is available and can reduce surface heating by changing the exposed surface area <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Photosynthetic stems allow for the reduction of leaves, which significantly reduces transpiration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Stomata reduced in number and/or sunken in stem epidermis reduces transpiration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reflective or light-coloured stem surface/spines/hairs reduce heat absorption and therefore evaporation <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award marks for other valid adaptations of stems for water conservation if clearly explained.</note>

questions.specification:
The table provides information about two greenhouse gases found in the Earth's atmosphere.

| Greenhouse Gas | Relative warming potential (100-year, $CO_2 = 1$) | Concentration in the atmosphere (ppm) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Methane ($CH_4$) | 28 | 1.9 |
| Carbon dioxide ($CO_2$) | 1 | 412.0 |

Which statement best explains why carbon dioxide has a larger overall effect on global warming than methane?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D4.3 Climate change > D4.3.1 Anthropogenic causes of climate change
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D4.3.1 Anthropogenic causes of climate change
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Compares methane and carbon dioxide using relative warming potential and atmospheric concentration to explain overall impact on global warming—core to anthropogenic causes of climate change and greenhouse gases.

options.content: Its concentration in the atmosphere is significantly higher.
options.markscheme: Correct. Despite lower relative warming potential per molecule, $CO_2$ is far more abundant (412.0 ppm vs 1.9 ppm), giving a larger overall effect.

options.content: It is more efficient at absorbing long-wave radiation per molecule.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. The table indicates methane has the higher relative warming potential per molecule (28 vs 1).

options.content: It is the only greenhouse gas that persists in the atmosphere for more than a year.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. This is not supported by the data, and methane can persist for more than a year.

options.content: It reflects a larger proportion of incoming short-wave solar radiation.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Greenhouse gases warm mainly by absorbing outgoing long-wave (infrared) radiation, not by reflecting short-wave radiation.

questions.specification: The greenhouse effect is influenced by the specific properties of various atmospheric gases. In addition to infrared absorption, which factor determines a gas’s overall contribution to global warming?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D4.3 Climate change > D4.3.1 Anthropogenic causes of climate change
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D4.3.1 Anthropogenic causes of climate change
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks what determines a greenhouse gas’s overall contribution to global warming besides IR absorption—this aligns with understanding drivers of climate change, including the role of atmospheric concentrations of greenhouse gases.

options.content: The density of the gas relative to the surrounding air
options.markscheme: The density of a gas determines its buoyancy or layering in the atmosphere, but it does not dictate its ability to trap heat or contribute to the greenhouse effect.

options.content: The concentration of the gas in the atmosphere
options.markscheme: The total impact of a greenhouse gas depends on two main factors: its ability to absorb long-wave (infrared) radiation and its abundance/concentration in the atmosphere.

options.content: The ability of the gas to reflect incoming short-wave radiation
options.markscheme: Reflecting short-wave radiation (albedo) would actually have a cooling effect; greenhouse gases work by absorbing long-wave radiation emitted from the Earth's surface.

options.content: The rate at which the gas is fixed by soil bacteria
options.markscheme: While some gases like nitrogen are fixed by bacteria, this process is part of the nitrogen cycle and is not a determinant of a gas's greenhouse warming potential.

***[Max 2 Marks]***

parts.content: Invasive species often cause a decline in local biodiversity. Outline the characteristics of alien species that allow them to successfully invade an ecosystem and outcompete endemic species.

- May possess superior defenses or weapons (e.g., toxins) to which native species have no immunity  <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Able to outcompete endemic species for limited resources (e.g., food, light, or space) through competitive exclusion  <marks>1 mark</marks>
- May be more efficient at resource acquisition than native counterparts  <marks>1 mark</marks>

***[Max 3 Marks]***

parts.content: Explain how various adaptations (structural, physiological, and behavioural) support survival in extreme environments.

- thick fur or blubber provides insulation against cold (structural) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- antifreeze proteins prevent ice crystal formation in body fluids (physiological) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- desert animals produce highly concentrated urine to conserve water (physiological) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- salt glands allow for the excretion of excess salt without water loss (structural/physiological) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- countercurrent heat exchange minimizes heat loss to the environment (physiological) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- behavioural adaptations like burrowing or nocturnal activity avoid temperature extremes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- extremophile enzymes are adapted to tolerate pH or temperature extremes (physiological) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- efficient respiration adaptations, such as high lung surface area, support high-altitude survival <marks>1 mark</marks>
- small or large surface area to volume ratios (SA:V) help regulate temperature depending on climate (structural) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe two types of adaptations that help organisms survive in cold environments.
parts.markscheme:
- Identifies a structural adaptation for cold environments (e.g. thick fur, blubber/fat layer, small surface area to volume ratio) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describes how the named structural adaptation reduces heat loss/increases insulation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identifies a physiological adaptation for cold environments (e.g. hibernation, antifreeze proteins, counter-current heat exchange) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describes how the named physiological adaptation conserves energy/reduces heat loss/maintains core temperature <marks>1 mark</marks>

<Note>
Award marks for other valid adaptations such as:
- Counter-current heat exchange
- Migration
- Huddling behaviour
</Note>

parts.content: Describe how behavioural adaptations can increase survival in cold environments.
parts.markscheme:
- Names an appropriate behavioural adaptation (e.g. migration, huddling, basking, seeking shelter/burrowing, reduced activity) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describes how the behaviour reduces heat loss (e.g. reduces exposed surface area, reduces convection/wind exposure) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describes how the behaviour reduces energy expenditure (e.g. reduces activity/metabolic demands; conserves energy when food is scarce) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Links behaviour to increased survival/reproductive success in cold conditions <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how thermoregulation in endotherms enables survival in cold environments.
parts.markscheme:
- Endotherms maintain core body temperature using heat generated by metabolism (cell respiration) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Vasoconstriction reduces blood flow to skin/extremities and decreases heat loss <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Insulation (fur/feathers/blubber) traps air and reduces heat transfer by conduction/convection <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Counter-current heat exchange transfers heat from arterial to venous blood in limbs, reducing heat loss <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Shivering thermogenesis increases muscle activity to increase heat production <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Non-shivering thermogenesis (brown adipose tissue) increases heat production via increased respiration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Overall explanation links these mechanisms to maintenance of enzyme function/metabolic processes and survival in cold environments <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how the **leaf structure** of a cactus is adapted for water conservation.
parts.markscheme:
- Leaves are modified into spines or needle-like structures <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduced surface area of the leaves (spines) minimizes the area available for transpiration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Spines provide shade for the fleshy stem, reducing its surface temperature and evaporation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Spines create a boundary layer of still air around the plant, reducing the rate of water loss from the stem <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Spines lack functional stomata, preventing water loss through the leaf tissue <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Spines protect the plant from herbivores that would damage tissues and cause water loss <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award marks for clear explanations that link the structural modification of the leaf (spine) to its role in reducing water loss. Max 4 marks.</note>

parts.content: Explain how the **root system** of a cactus is adapted for **obtaining water**.
parts.markscheme:
- Shallow or superficial roots are located near the soil surface <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Allows for the collection of water from light rainfall or dew before it evaporates from the soil <marks>1 mark</marks>
- A wide lateral spread / extensive horizontal network covers a large area around the plant <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Maximizes the surface area available for water absorption from the soil <marks>1 mark</marks>
- High root-to-shoot ratio provides an extensive collection network relative to the plant's water-losing surface area <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Roots can respond quickly to rainfall by growing new root hairs (rain roots) to increase absorption <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award marks for explanations that show how root distribution and structure facilitate the acquisition of water in arid environments. Max 4 marks.</note>

parts.content: Explain how the **stem** of a cactus is adapted for survival in dry environments.
parts.markscheme:
- Stem is thick/fleshy (succulent) and stores water in parenchyma cells/vacuoles <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Thick waxy cuticle on the stem reduces evaporation/transpiration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Stomata are fewer and/or sunken and/or open mainly at night (CAM physiology) to reduce water loss during gas exchange <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Stem has a low surface area to volume ratio (often cylindrical/rounded) to reduce water loss per unit volume <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Stem is photosynthetic (contains chlorophyll) so photosynthesis can occur while leaves are reduced to spines <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ribs/folds in the stem allow expansion when water is available and contraction during drought without damaging tissues <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Thickened epidermis/hypodermis reduces water loss and provides protection <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award marks for linked explanations focused on how stem structure/physiology reduces water loss and/or allows water storage; accept any 7 relevant points.</note>

parts.content: Compare the oxygen consumption of the Arctic fox and the desert fox at different temperatures.
parts.markscheme:
- At cold temperatures (-20°C and 0°C), the desert fox has a much higher oxygen consumption than the Arctic fox.
- At moderate temperatures (20°C), both species have lower oxygen consumption, but the Arctic fox consumes slightly less.
- At hot temperatures (40°C), the desert fox has the lowest oxygen consumption (0.80 mL O₂ g⁻¹ min⁻¹), while the Arctic fox’s oxygen consumption increases again (1.30 mL O₂ g⁻¹ min⁻¹).

Total: <marks>2 marks</marks> : <marks>1 mark</marks> stating differences in cold and warm temperatures, <marks>1 mark</marks> identifying overall trends in both species

parts.content: Explain how the differences in oxygen consumption relate to adaptations to their environments.
parts.markscheme:
- Arctic foxes have thick fur and a compact body shape that reduce heat loss; therefore they require a lower metabolic rate (lower oxygen consumption) at cold temperatures.
- Desert foxes have large ears and a leaner body that increase heat loss, which is advantageous for heat dissipation in hot environments but leads to greater heat loss in cold conditions; therefore oxygen consumption is higher at low temperatures to maintain body temperature.
- In hot conditions, the desert fox’s metabolic rate can be lower because it is adapted to prevent overheating, while the Arctic fox may show increased oxygen consumption at 40°C due to increased energetic costs of thermoregulation (e.g. cooling responses/heat stress) outside its usual cold range.

Total: <marks>2 marks</marks> : <marks>1 mark</marks> linking metabolic rates to temperature regulation, <marks>1 mark</marks> explaining how body structures aid adaptation
